items.title: USS Gerald R. Ford carrier departs Croatia
items.summary: WASHINGTON: The USS Gerald R. Ford has departed Croatia after a five-day port visit, the US Navy said Thursday without specifying where the world's largest aircraft carrier is headed next. The carrier "remains poised for full mission tasking in support of national objectives in any area of operation," according to the Navy, which said the ship "completed scheduled repairs and received supplies to sustain operations." The blaze injured two sailors and caused major damage to some 100 beds, according to the US military.

items.title: Vatican to compensate victims of abusive Peru Catholic group
items.summary: VATICAN CITY: The Vatican has announced plans to compensate victims of a Peru-based Catholic movement, which was dissolved last year following revelations of rampant sexual and psychological abuse by some of its leaders. Pope Francis ordered the closure of the ultra-conservative Sodalitium Christianae Vitae (SCV) a week before his death, after a seven-year Vatican investigation. The investigation also revealed financial mismanagement.

items.title: Defence minister vows action after assault on soldiers
items.summary: KUALA LUMPUR: Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin has vowed action over the assault of seven soldiers by an army corporal, resulting in one of them suffering brain damage. Khaled said his ministry will ensure that police receive the cooperation they need to complete their probe. We do not condone this (violence)," Khaled told the New Straits Times.

items.title: US-Iran conflict: What it means for Malaysians’ consumption habits
items.summary: How oil prices could affect household budgets Tensions in the Middle East have made oil prices more volatile, raising costs across supply chains. As fuel is used at every stage of food production — from fertiliser to transport — higher oil prices will eventually feed into food costs. Making early adjustments Even before prices rise further, some consumers are already adjusting.

items.title: Beyond trespassing claims, Orang Asli women stand at the heart of land rights struggle
items.summary: KUALA LUMPUR, April 3 — Among the 21 Orang Asli arrested in February for trespassing on a palm oil plantation near Kampung Jemeri in Kuala Rompin, Pahang, 17 were women. The company that was given permission to develop the land said the Orang Asli presence there was illegal. Women.

items.title: Barca crush Real Madrid to reach women's Champions League semis
items.summary: BARCELONA: Barcelona demolished rivals Real Madrid 6-0 on Thursday to progress to the women's Champions League semi-finals 12-2 on aggregate. Three-time winners Barca reached an eighth consecutive semi-final, with winger Caroline Graham Hansen netting twice and Putellas also on the scoresheet in the rout. Two-time Ballon d'Or winner Putellas put Barca ahead on her 500th appearance for the club early on, turning home a rebound after Misa Rodriguez saved from Ewa Pajor.

items.title: Trump sacks attorney general, replaces with ex-personal lawyer
items.summary: WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump fired Attorney General Pam Bondi on Thursday after months of strained relations and named his former personal lawyer to serve as the acting chief of the Justice Department. "Pam Bondi is a Great American Patriot and a loyal friend, who faithfully served as my Attorney General over the past year," Trump said on Truth Social. Bondi has also reportedly drawn Trump's ire by falling short with efforts to prosecute perceived Trump opponents such as former FBI director James Comey and New York Attorney General Letitia James.

items.title: 'Artemis II' lifts off for moon mission
items.summary: Four astronauts blasted off on Wednesday on Nasa's Artemis II mission, a high-stakes voyage around the moon that marks the United States' boldest step yet towards returning humans to the lunar surface later this decade in a race with China. Nasa's Space Launch System (SLS) rocket, topped with its Orion crew capsule, roared to life just before sunset at the agency's Kennedy Space Center here, carrying its debut crew — three US astronauts and a Canadian — into Earth orbit. If the mission proceeds as planned, Nasa astronauts ‌Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover and Christina Koch, plus Canadian Space Agency astronaut Jeremy Hansen, will fly around the moon and back in their nearly 10-day expedition, putting the spacecraft through its paces while venturing deeper into space than humans have ever gone.

items.title: Amazon eyes US$9b Globalstar deal to rival Starlink, says FT
items.summary: NEW YORK: Amazon is in talks to buy satellite telecom group Globalstar as it ramps up efforts to build its own low-earth-orbit satellite business to rival SpaceX's Starlink, the Financial Times (FT) reported on Wednesday, citing people familiar with the matter. One complicating factor has been Apple's ownership of a 20 per cent stake in Globalstar, necessitating negotiations between Amazon and Apple, the report said. The reported deal comes as Amazon deploys Leo, formerly known as Project Kuiper, involving 3,200 satellites.

items.title: 'AI is no replacement for human judgment'
items.summary: The legal profession must guard against becoming overly dependent on artificial intelligence (AI), said Chief Justice Datuk Seri Wan Ahmad Farid Wan Salleh, cautioning that technology should not erode the human element. "Technology makes things faster, but I dare say it can make us less human. Present were Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim, Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said and Universiti Malaya pro-chancellor Tan Sri Zainun Ali.

items.title: Nga: All 34 'sick' PR1MA projects completed
items.summary: A total of 34 previously delayed PR1MA housing projects have been completed, allowing 22,925 buyers to finally receive their homes after years of waiting. Housing and Local Government Minister Nga Kor Ming said the milestone was marked by the handover of keys for the final two projects — Residensi Klebang 2 and Residensi Bukit Katil. "The Madani government inherited 34 sick PR1MA projects nationwide, involving 22,925 housing units.

items.title: How high can oil prices go after fresh surge?
items.summary: LONDON: Having once more soared beyond US$110 per barrel on Thursday, could oil prices reach new records beyond US$150 as some analysts believe? - US$200 oil? French bank Societe Generale said US$150 is a "credible" outcome on a prolonged war, while most analysts estimate crude hitting US$130-US$140.

items.title: Couple suffer burns in Kuala Krai shop fire
items.summary: KUALA KRAI: A couple suffered burns after their shop selling Chinese religious items at Central Park here caught fire last night. Kuala Krai Fire and Rescue Station (BBP) chief Nik Ahmad Afsham Nik Pa said the department received an emergency call at about 8.30pm before dispatching a firefighting team. He said a total of 21 personnel from BBP Kuala Krai, BBP Sungai Durian and the Guchil Volunteer Fire Team were involved in the operation.

items.title: China condemns US‑Israeli strikes on Iran as violation of international law
items.summary: BEIJING: China's foreign minister Wang Yi said on Thursday that US and Israeli attacks on Iran were a violation of international law, Chinese state media reported, as he held calls with his EU, German and Saudi counterparts. Wang also stressed that the UN Security Council (UNSC) – of which China is a permanent member – "should prevent the escalation of conflict, and not legalise unauthorised military actions", state broadcaster CCTV said, as the international body debates a draft resolution on the use of force in the Hormuz strait. "The military attack launched by the United States and Israel against Iran was not authorised by the UNSC and clearly violated international law", Wang told Germany's foreign minister Johann Wadephul in a phone call Thursday, CCTV reported.

items.title: 225 Malaysian medical personnel to depart for haj duties next week
items.summary: KUALA LUMPUR: A total of 225 Malaysian medical personnel will depart for the Holy Land next week to serve during the 1447H/2026 haj season. Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad said, despite ongoing geopolitical challenges in the Middle East, haj operations remain well-managed through strong diplomacy by the government under Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim and cooperation with the Saudi Arabian government. "The absence of a Tabung Haji treatment centre means pilgrims will be referred directly to Saudi government hospitals, requiring personnel to demonstrate proactiveness, patience and diplomacy," he said in a Facebook post.

items.title: Girl found dead at home in Sg Buloh, 3 questioned
items.summary: KUALA LUMPUR: A 13-year-old girl was found dead at a home in Sungai Buloh yesterday. Sungai Buloh district police chief superintendent Mohd Hafiz Muhammad Nor said police received information on the victim being found unconscious at 6.27pm. He said a medical officer from Sungai Buloh Hospital who arrived at the scene confirmed that the victim had died.

items.title: UN accuses Israel of impunity in deaths of Lebanese journalists, demands accountability
items.summary: GENEVA: UN experts on Thursday called for an international investigation into the death of three Lebanese journalists in an Israeli strike, saying Israel had not provided "credible evidence" of their alleged links to armed groups. The three journalists, including Ali Shoeib, a star correspondent for Al Manar channel of Hizbollah, which is at war with Israel, were killed on March 28 in an Israeli strike in southern Lebanon. According to the experts, Israel's only so-called "evidence" for its claims was a photoshopped image of the journalist.

items.title: War meant to break Iran may leave it stronger
items.summary: IF United States President Donald Trump ends the war with Iran without a deal, he risks leaving Teheran with a stranglehold over Middle East energy supplies and Gulf Arab oil and gas producers grappling with the fallout of a conflict they did not start or shape. Instead of crushing Iran's theocratic rulers, it could leave them stronger, emboldened by surviving weeks of US-Israeli attacks, firing on Arab Gulf states and rattling global energy markets by effectively shutting the Strait of Hormuz. "He (Trump) might stop the war, but that doesn't mean Iran will."

items.title: Malaysia must get serious about intoxicated drivers
items.summary: DRIVING under the influence of alcohol or drugs is no longer just a reckless act — it is murder in motion. Malaysians are tired of reading headlines about yet another drunk driver mowing down a motorcyclist, or a speeding car ploughing into unsuspecting pedestrians. The law is clear: driving while intoxicated is prohibited.

items.title: Home Ministry assesses scenarios as US-Iran conflict intensifies
items.summary: PUTRAJAYA: The Home Ministry is reviewing possible scenarios arising from the United States–Iran conflict, including implications for domestic security, cross‑border movement, economic stability and enforcement agency preparedness. Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail said he chaired a meeting with the ministry's management and its agencies under a Strategic Meeting on the Impact of the US–Iran Conflict to assess developments surrounding the situation. "This morning, I chaired a meeting with the Home Ministry's management and its agencies under the Strategic Meeting on the Impact of the US–Iran Conflict to assess developments in this conflict, which is increasingly drawing global attention," he said in a Facebook post today.

items.title: NST Leader: Drink-driving claims another victim while enforcement falters
items.summary: THERE has been another death caused by an intoxicated driver. The 28-year-old driver allegedly collided head-on with the motorcycle ridden by Amirul Hafiz Omar while trying to overtake several vehicles at high speed in Klang, Selangor, on Sunday. In Australia, for example, courts issue compensation orders for damage to road infrastructure.

items.title: HR ministry reviews maternity leave allowance under Perkeso
items.summary: SUBANG JAYA: The Human Resources Ministry is reviewing a proposal to introduce a maternity leave assistance allowance through the Social Security Organisation (Perkeso). Its minister, Datuk Seri R. Ramanan, said the proposed allowance, to be implemented under Perkeso's insurance system, is expected to benefit more than 100,000 mothers nationwide. He said the ministry is also developing further measures to enhance workers' welfare, which will be implemented in stages.

items.title: Calls grow to tackle 'Jewish terrorism' as West Bank attacks intensify
items.summary: JERUSALEM: Violence by extremist Israeli settlers against Palestinians in the occupied West Bank, denounced as "Jewish terrorism" by politicians and former Israeli security officials, has surged since the start of the war with Iran, with rights groups alleging that perpetrators operate in near-total impunity. Assaults by violent settlers against Palestinians have persisted for years, often to the indifference of mainstream Israeli society. - 'IDEOLOGICAL SUPPORT' - Israel has occupied the West Bank since 1967.

items.title: Kundasang cable car proposal to undergo strict review
items.summary: KOTA KINABALU: The proposal to build the Kinabalu cable car project in Kundasang will be evaluated carefully and comprehensively, said Sabah Tourism, Culture and Environment Minister Datuk Jafry Ariffin. He said the assessment would be guided by the principle of "conservation first" as the primary basis of consideration. He said Mount Kinabalu is not merely a tourism asset, but a global treasure as a Unesco World Heritage Site, with exceptionally high biodiversity value and ecological significance.

items.title: Iraq begins oil exports on tankers through Syria
items.summary: BAGHDAD: Iraq has begun exporting crude using tanker trucks through Syria, its oil ministry said, as an official said oil revenue last month dropped more than 70 per cent compared to February. Like other exporters in the oil-rich region, Iraq has been left scrambling for alternative routes, and in a statement late Wednesday, the oil ministry said it "has begun exporting oil by tanker truck through neighbouring Syria." Syria, it said, would "ensure the safe passage" of the oil, and added exports will "gradually" increase.

items.title: Weekly watch: Asean fuel prices
items.summary: KUALA LUMPUR: Fuel prices across Asean remain uneven this week, with Malaysia recording among the lowest retail rates for both petrol and diesel, supported by subsidy mechanisms and administered pricing. In Brunei, fuel prices remain significantly lower than those of most Asean peers, underpinned by its status as an oil-producing country with long-standing domestic subsidy policies. In Timor-Leste, fuel prices are subject to government-imposed caps aimed at stabilising retail costs amid global oil market volatility.

items.title: Tesla quarterly auto sales rise 6.3pct but miss estimates
items.summary: NEW YORK: Tesla reported higher first-quarter auto sales Thursday, but missed analyst estimates. Analysts had projected sales of 365,645, according to consensus figures on Tesla's website. But Tesla enjoyed a brief surge in sales in the third quarter, as the US phase-out of a consumer tax credit lifted sales.

items.title: Foreign vehicles entering Brunei must have three-quarters tank fuel
items.summary: KUALA LUMPUR: All foreign-registered vehicles must have at least three-quarters of a tank of fuel before being allowed to enter Brunei, effective tomorrow. Brunei's Energy Department at the Prime Minister's Office said in a statement today that vehicles failing to meet the requirement will be denied entry, except for official government vehicles granted exemption. Foreign-registered vehicles are also only allowed to purchase Shell V-Power Gasoline or Shell V-Power Diesel at prevailing market prices.

items.title: Putin, Saudi prince seek more efforts to end Mideast war
items.summary: MOSCOW: Russian President Vladimir Putin on Thursday called for intensifying political and diplomatic efforts to end the Middle East war during a phone call with Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman. Facing isolation from the West after sending troops to Ukraine, Putin has sought to strengthen partnerships in the Middle East, maintaining close relations both with Iran and the Gulf monarchies. The call comes after Ukraine – fending off Russia's full-scale assault since 2022 – signed an air defence deal with Saudi Arabia as the Gulf nation grapples with Iranian drone attacks.

items.title: Tahfiz dorm fire: 15 students, 3 teachers escape unhurt
items.summary: Principal Mohamad Halimi Kolan said he heard cracking sounds from the upper floor housing the dormitory and hall before asking a teacher to check. "I heard cracking sounds from the upper floor which houses the dormitory and hall. Meanwhile, the Johor Fire and Rescue Department's Operations Centre said it received an emergency call at 4.35pm before dispatching a fire engine from the Larkin Fire and Rescue Station to the location.

items.title: Man held for using multiple ICs to buy over 100 litres of petrol
items.summary: RANTAU PANJANG: Police have arrested a man for allegedly using the identity cards of four people to purchase more than 100 litres of petrol yesterday. Kelantan police chief Datuk Mohd Yusoff Mamat said the suspect was detained while filling up at a petrol station in Tumpat. "When apprehended, we found found four identity cards in his possession.

items.title: Pop Mart shares plunge as Labubu craze fades
items.summary: BEIJING: Shares of Pop Mart International Group have plunged more than 30 per cent over five sessions through March 31, as concerns mount over its heavy reliance on hit character Labubu. According to a Bloomberg report, the decline extends losses from its August peak to nearly 60 per cent, erasing about US$33 billion (RM156 billion) in market value. Labubu's surge in 2025 — which drove a roughly 300 per cent rally in shares — had marked a rare instance of China's soft power gaining traction in Western markets.

items.title: WFH for civil servants: Those in FT, Selangor, state capitals with commutes over 8km eligible from April 15
items.summary: PUTRAJAYA, April 2 — Federal civil servants stationed in Kuala Lumpur, Putrajaya as well as Selangor and all state capitals with a one-way commute exceeding eight kilometres are eligible to work from home (WFH) beginning April 15. “The Human Resource Services Circular (PPSM) SR.4.1.2: Work from Home Policy provides for the policy, conditions and regulations governing WFH. It stated that several sectors are exempted, including security and defence services such as the Malaysian Armed Forces (ATM), Royal Malaysia Police (PDRM), Fire and Rescue Department, Prisons Department, Malaysian Maritime Enforcement Agency, Border Control and Protection Agency (AKPS) and Immigration Department.

items.title: Global energy crisis: Anwar convenes special meeting with state leaders to ensure supply stability
items.summary: KUALA LUMPUR, April 2 — Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim today chaired a special meeting of Menteri Besar and Chief Ministers to review developments in the global energy crisis following the conflict in West Asia. In a Facebook post, Anwar said the government’s approach is grounded in data and current realities, ensuring state leaders act in alignment, in a coordinated manner and based on shared facts with the federal government. He stressed that the government is undertaking comprehensive scenario planning to address various contingencies, including ensuring an uninterrupted national energy supply.

items.title: Malaysia’s oil supply stable, govt has contingency plans, says DPM Fadillah
items.summary: KUALA LUMPUR, April 2 — Malaysia’s petroleum supply remains stable and sufficient, and the government has prepared contingency plans to ensure continued supply under any circumstances, said Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Fadillah Yusof. He said the plan includes sourcing supplies from alternative markets if the ongoing global energy crisis — triggered by conflict in West Asia — causes prolonged disruptions. “Nevertheless, we must remain vigilant and take precautionary measures if this crisis continues,” he said during a Global Energy Crisis Briefing broadcast live on local television today.

items.title: Police seize RM1.5m in non-halal chicken and pork in Kampung Baru Subang, 32 detained
items.summary: Bukit Aman Internal Security and Public Order Department (JKDNKA) Wildlife Crime Bureau (WCB) Commander, SAC Mohd Zaki Ashar, said the seizures followed raids on three separate premises in the area. Other agencies involved were the Inland Revenue Board (LHDN), the Malaysian Quarantine and Inspection Services Department (MAQIS), and the Royal Malaysian Customs Department. He said several agencies involved in the operation also issued compounds for various offences detected at the locations.

items.title: Penang ramps up AED deployment in public areas to boost cardiac arrest survival rates
items.summary: State exco for youth, sports and health Daniel Gooi Zi Sen said this year, Penang Island City Council (MBPP) will be installing an additional 71 AED devices in public spaces. “Similarly, the Seberang Perai City Council (MBSP) also did a procurement of AED devices to be deployed in public spaces this year,” he said in a press conference after a Heroes for Life handover ceremony in Komtar today. In addition to AED devices installed by the city councils, he said there are also AED devices installed by private corporations and developers.

items.title: Johor police to extend remand for trio in TikTok royal insult probe
items.summary: JOHOR BAHRU, April 2 — Police will seek to extend the remand order for three family members, including a teenager, arrested for allegedly disseminating TikTok content that insulted Islam and the Johor royal institution. Johor police chief Datuk Ab Rahaman Arsad said the current remand for the two women and one man, aged between 17 and 49, ends tomorrow. “So far we have received up to three police reports in Johor regarding this case,” he told reporters at the Johor police contingent headquarters here today.

items.title: Sabah mulls Mount Kinabalu cable car proposal, stresses conservation-first approach
items.summary: KOTA KINABALU, April 2 — The Sabah government will consider a proposal to build a cable car system at Kinabalu Park, on the foothills of the famed Mount Kinabalu, considering its potential economic boost and tourist appeal. However, State Tourism, Culture and Environment Minister Jafry Ariffin said any development must prioritise environmental conservation and the protection of the park’s Unesco World Heritage status. He said that Mount Kinabalu, as a Unesco World Heritage Site, holds high biodiversity value and ecological importance, and therefore any development must comply with strict standards, including a comprehensive Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA).

items.title: Fuel price adjustments carried out in controlled manner to safeguard public welfare, says DPM Fadillah
items.summary: KUALA LUMPUR, April 2 — The adjustment to non-subsidised fuel prices is being done in a controlled manner to reflect global market realities without burdening the public, Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Fadillah Yusof said. Fadillah, who is also Minister of Energy Transition and Water Transformation (PETRA), said the approach aims to balance global market pressures with safeguards for households. This enables real-time feedback and swift policy adjustments to ensure supply stability and market order,” he said at the Global Energy Crisis Briefing, broadcast live by local television stations today.

items.title: Investors holding firm: Bank Negara says steady foreign projects key to Malaysia weathering Middle East turmoil
items.summary: KUALA LUMPUR, April 2 — Bank Negara Malaysia (BNM) Deputy Governor Datuk Marzunisham Omar said today multinational firms have signalled their intention to keep all projects in Malaysia going, a key factor that is helping the South-east Asian nation withstand the impact from a worsening crisis in the Middle East. The assertion, made at a forum before the country’s business circle, came as some expressed concerns that the war could hurt Malaysia’s export-driven economy. Marzunisham said Malaysia is weathering the energy and supply shock from a position of strength, as he urged businesses to remain optimistic and focus on what makes the country strong.

items.title: Global energy crisis: Putrajaya to cap air-conditioning at offices to 24°C, tighten energy use in govt buildings, says DPM Fadillah
items.summary: KUALA LUMPUR, April 2 — Energy controls in government buildings will be tightened, including setting air-conditioning temperatures at no lower than 24°Celsius, following the global energy crisis, Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Fadillah Yusof said. “As a proactive response to the global energy crisis, the government will implement several energy-saving measures at the national level,” he said at the Global Energy Crisis Briefing, broadcast live by local television stations today. The ongoing global energy crisis, following conflict in West Asia, has had a direct impact on energy supply and prices worldwide,” he said.

items.title: Toxic beauty: MOH bans two cosmetic products over mercury and other poisons
items.summary: It said in a statement today that the two products contained prohibited substances such as mercury, hydroquinone, tretinoin and betamethasone 17-valerate. “Mercury can also cause rashes, irritation and other changes to the skin,” said the statement. The MOH said the use of cosmetics containing these substances without professional health supervision can cause various side effects.

items.title: Power outage stalls Ampang/Sri Petaling line, Rapid Rail rolls out shuttle buses
items.summary: KUALA LUMPUR, April 2 — Train services on the Ampang/Sri Petaling route are experiencing delays due to a power outage between Awan Besar Station and Sri Petaling Station. For alternative train services, trains from Putra Heights Station will turn back at Kinrara BK5 Station with an expected frequency of seven minutes, while trains from Sentul Timur Station will turn back at Bandar Tasik Selatan Station with a frequency of 10 minutes. In addition, shuttle trains are provided between Bandar Tasik Selatan Station and Awan Besar Station and between Kinrara BK5 Station and Awan Besar Station with an expected frequency of 15 minutes.

items.title: Rider’s leg severed in early morning KL crash, Honda City driver arrested
items.summary: KUALA LUMPUR, April 2 — A 24-year-old motorcyclist had his leg severed in a horrific crash after a car allegedly ran a red light along Jalan Sultan Ismail here early this morning. Kuala Lumpur Traffic Investigation and Enforcement Department chief ACP Zamzuri Isa said the collision occurred at about 4.27am, Free Malaysia Today reported.. Both victims were rushed to Kuala Lumpur Hospital for treatment.

items.title: Heat check: Environment Ministry to brief Cabinet on dry spell, cloud seeding in play
items.summary: KUALA LUMPUR, April 2 — The Ministry of Natural Resources and Environmental Sustainability (NRES) plans to submit a detailed weather forecast report on the current dry and hot weather to the Cabinet next week. Minister of Natural Resources and Environmental Sustainability Datuk Seri Arthur Joseph Kurup said the move was to enable the Cabinet to discuss if there was an urgent need to intervene, including cloud seeding operations. “However, NRES will always provide forecast information from time to time,” he said when commenting on NRES’s latest preparations in dealing with hot weather.

items.title: Sabah Tourism Board: Preparedness is key, destination remains compelling despite global jitters
items.summary: KUALA LUMPUR, April 2 — The Sabah Tourism Board (STB) is establishing a crisis response unit to strengthen preparedness and ensure swift, coordinated action in the face of global uncertainties affecting the tourism sector. Assistant Minister to the Sabah Chief Minister Datuk Joniston Bangkuai said the move reflects a proactive approach to managing risks arising from geopolitical developments and economic challenges, while maintaining confidence in Sabah as a destination. Meanwhile, STB Chief Executive Officer Julinus Jeffery Jimit said about 60 per cent of Sabah’s visitor arrivals are domestic, with China remaining the main international market.

items.title: ‘Justice delayed is justice denied’: Road Safety Council urges dedicated tribunals for crash victims
items.summary: KUALA LUMPUR, April 2 — The Road Safety Council has urged the government and the judiciary to consider establishing Special Road Accident Courts nationwide to expedite cases involving fatalities and serious injuries. Council member Lee Lam Thye said dedicated courts or tribunals would enable faster case resolution, ensure timely compensation and strengthen accountability among road users. “Justice delayed is justice denied,” he said in a statement, emphasising that prolonged legal processes deny victims timely closure and fair compensation.

items.title: Penang Port Commission ramps up safeguards as global energy crisis rattles supply chains
items.summary: GEORGE TOWN, April 2 — The Penang Port Commission (PPC) has stepped up efforts to safeguard supply chain continuity and ensure stable port operations amid the global energy crisis stemming from conflicts in West Asia and volatile oil prices. PPC chairman Datuk Yeoh Soon Hin said the current global situation has significant implications for logistics costs, energy price stability and overall supply chain resilience, placing pressure on ports and industrial activities worldwide. Additionally, Yeoh said the PPC has adopted prudent financial strategies, reinforcing budget discipline and cost control measures to ensure efficient resource allocation without compromising service standards.

items.title: What is Malaysia Vision Valley 2.0? Inside Negeri Sembilan’s six-zone economic mega-project
items.summary: KUALA LUMPUR, April 2 — You may have heard the name, but what exactly is Malaysia Vision Valley 2.0 (MVV 2.0)? Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir (left) and Negeri Sembilan Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Aminuddin Harun speak to reporters during a visit to the construction site of the new highway linking Nilai, Labu, and Bandar Enstek in Nilai on April 2, 2026. Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir (left) and Negeri Sembilan Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Aminuddin Harun (centre) inspect plans during a site visit in Nilai on April 2, 2026.

items.title: Director‑general and deputy of Environment Department expected in court over corruption case tied to e‑waste
items.summary: PUTRAJAYA, April 2 — The director-general of the Department of Environment (DOE) and its deputy director-general are expected to be charged at the Kuala Lumpur Sessions Court tomorrow in connection with an investigation into electronic waste management. Confirming the matter, Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) Chief Commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said the charges would proceed following approval from the public prosecutor and the Attorney General. Azam had earlier said the MACC would intensify its efforts to combat corruption involving the management of e-waste and plastic waste, following developments implicating senior officers within the department.

items.title: Zafrul: Govt needs standby stimulus ready to tackle sustained Middle East crisis
items.summary: KUALA LUMPUR, April 2 — The government must have a structured stimulus plan on standby, ready to be activated if the global energy crisis continues to exert pressure on Malaysia’s economy and the people’s cost of living, said former minister Datuk Seri Tengku Zafrul Aziz In a statement posted on social media, the former minister of investment, trade, and industry stressed that the current global conflict has created a “new normal” that will place sustained pressure on energy costs and supply chains for a prolonged period. He said that while additional measures may not be required today, the government must be prepared to act swiftly when circumstances change. “The next stimulus or additional initiative need not necessarily be large, but it must be structured, phased, and activated when necessary,” Tengku Zafrul wrote.

items.title: Johor police bust drug syndicate, seize narcotics worth RM1.2m
items.summary: JOHOR BAHRU, April 2 — Police have crippled a drug trafficking syndicate following two raids here on Monday and Tuesday, arresting six individuals and seizing various narcotics worth RM1.24 million. Johor police chief Datuk Ab Rahaman Arsad said the suspects — four local men, one Singaporean man, and one local woman, aged between 31 and 60 — were arrested in separate operations in Taman Megah Ria and Taman Sri Tebrau. “One of the suspects, a local man, is believed to be the syndicate’s coordinator, while two others were identified as suppliers,” he told reporters at the Johor police contingent headquarters today.

items.title: Ahmad Zahid: Stop blaming government for fuel prices, Malaysia in ‘crisis preparation mode’
items.summary: PUTRAJAYA, April 2 — Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi said the country is currently in crisis preparedness mode following global uncertainties which affect fuel prices. Therefore, Ahmad Zahid who is also the minister of rural and regional development, asked all parties not to point fingers at the government regarding the fuel price hike. He said the current situation was caused by the global crisis and required the cooperation of all parties to deal with it together.

items.title: Economy minister: Malaysian Vision Valley Project 60pc complete, targeted to finish this year despite delays
items.summary: NILAI, April 2 — The Malaysian Vision Valley (MVV) project is showing renewed progress after previous delays and is now targeted for completion within the year, said Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir. After a site visit here today, the minister confirmed that the project, a massive state-led economic growth corridor in Negeri Sembilan, has surpassed 60 per cent completion following the appointment of a rescue contractor. “Taking into account the challenges involved, the target remains to complete the project within this year, with emphasis placed on expediting the works,” he saiid, adding that the project’s cost remains within the existing contract terms.

items.title: We can’t pass on rising costs: Malaysia’s tourism industry pleads for urgent govt aid, subsidies
items.summary: The Malaysian Tourism Federation (MTF) said the Malaysian government needs to urgently help the local tourism industry now, as the tourism operators’ financial burden continues to increase. MTF president Sri Ganesh Michiel explained how Malaysia’s travel industry works, where tour package prices are locked in advance: Malaysia’s travel agencies design travel packages and then negotiate and sell them to travel agencies abroad. Sri Ganesh Michiel, president of Malaysian Tourism Federation, should immediately give out fuel subsidies to tourism transport operators, as the local tourism industry may otherwise be forced to absorb higher costs and bear losses.

items.title: Penang KPDN seizes RM415,877 in subsidised diesel, steps up crackdown on misuse
items.summary: SEBERANG PERAI, April 2 — The Ministry of Domestic Trade and Cost of Living (KPDN) in Penang has recorded eight cases of misappropriated subsidised diesel, with total seizures amounting to RM415,877.95. Penang KPDN director S. Jegan said the cases, recorded between January and April this year, resulted in five arrests. KPDN Penang also received four public complaints related to the misuse of subsidised fuel.

items.title: Azam stays tight-lipped on Bloomberg report: ‘I don’t want to comment’
items.summary: PUTRAJAYA, April 2 — Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki today declined to comment on recent reports by Bloomberg concerning allegations linked to his shareholdings. Bloomberg previously published an investigative report highlighting Azam’s shareholdings in several listed companies, alongside allegations that senior MACC officers had colluded with a group of businessmen in attempts to take control of targeted firms. Azam and the MACC have denied any wrongdoing.

items.title: MACC chief urges public to see beyond ‘bad apples’ in law enforcement agencies
items.summary: PUTRAJAYA, April 2 — Public perception towards enforcement officers must change, Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said today, noting that the actions of a few “bad apples” often overshadow the majority who serve with integrity. Speaking at the Multiagency Taskforce Engagement Session at MACC headquarters here, Azam said negative perceptions frequently arise whenever issues of corruption or misconduct surface, even though many officers continue to carry out their duties professionally. “Enforcement personnel are frequently associated with corruption whenever any issue arises.

items.title: MACC chief: Corporate criminals manipulating shares openly, agencies must keep up
items.summary: PUTRAJAYA, April 2 — Share manipulation and other forms of corporate crime are increasingly being carried out openly, Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said today, stressing the need for authorities to step up efforts to detect such activities. Speaking at the Multiagency Taskforce Engagement Session at the MACC headquarters here, Azam said corporate criminals are using complex structures and professional networks to hide fraudulent activities, including tax evasion, money laundering and manipulation of shareholdings. He said corporate criminals also often rely on networks of professionals such as accountants and lawyers, as well as insiders within relevant agencies, to facilitate illegal activities while reducing the risk of detection.
